---
title: "System Design Behind Multi-Conference Video Calls - WebRTC vs SFU vs MCU"
author: "Piyush Garg"
author_url: "https://www.youtube.com/@piyushgargdev"
date: 2026-06-29
type: video
category: "System Design"
tags: [webrtc, system-design, sfu, mcu, video-streaming, networking]
video_url: "https://www.youtube.com/embed/Zaz6hYVm-WE"
source_url: "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Zaz6hYVm-WE"
estimated_read_time: 15
is_own_work: false
gists:
  short: "Multi-party video calls like Zoom and Google Meet can't use simple WebRTC peer-to-peer — that only works for two users. The naive fix (Mesh P2P) has every peer connect to every other peer directly, which becomes unmanageable past 4-5 users. MCU (Multipoint Control Unit) solves this with a central server that merges all streams into one combined feed, but it's extremely CPU-intensive and kills client-side flexibility. The industry standard is SFU (Selective Forwarding Unit): each peer sends one stream to the server, which forwards every participant's raw stream separately back to all others. The client handles rendering — enabling individual muting, pinning, and resizing per user. MediaSoup is a popular open-source library for building your own SFU."
  long: "## What is a Multi-Conference Application\n\nApplications like Zoom, Google Meet, and Discord allow more than two people to participate in a real-time video call simultaneously. Designing these systems is non-trivial — they require transmitting live audio and video with minimal latency, and the architecture must scale as participants grow.\n\nEach participant in these systems is called a **peer**. The core challenge is: how do you efficiently move audio and video streams between many peers at once?\n\n## Peer-to-Peer (WebRTC)\n\nWebRTC (Web Real-Time Communication) enables direct peer-to-peer connections between two users. It runs over UDP for speed, requires no server in between, and is essentially free — you only pay for your own internet bandwidth. This works perfectly when exactly two people are on a call.\n\nHowever, WebRTC was designed for one-to-one connections. The moment a third peer wants to join, things get complicated.\n\n## Mesh P2P — Scaling WebRTC Naively\n\nTo support multiple peers using WebRTC, you can use a **Mesh P2P** architecture: every peer opens a direct WebRTC connection to every other peer. With three peers, that means three connections. With four peers, six connections. The number of connections grows as N×(N-1)/2.\n\nThis technically works but falls apart quickly. When a new peer joins, every existing peer must establish a new connection with them. Each peer has to upload their stream to every other peer individually, multiplying bandwidth usage. With 10-12 participants, a single peer might have 11 active connections — crushing their upload bandwidth and CPU. Debugging is difficult, and the setup is prone to frequent crashes. Mesh P2P is not a viable production approach.\n\n## MCU — Multipoint Control Unit\n\nThe industry's first real solution was the **MCU (Multipoint Control Unit)**. Instead of peers connecting to each other, all peers connect to a central server over WebRTC (peer-to-server, which is still a valid WebRTC connection). Each peer sends their audio and video stream to the server.\n\nThe MCU server then **combines all incoming streams into a single mixed stream** — one video feed containing all participants — and broadcasts that back to every peer. This is the architecture used in multi-host YouTube livestreams, where viewers see a single combined video of all guests.\n\nThe problem: merging multiple real-time video streams into one is extremely CPU-intensive. It introduces noticeable lag, drives up server costs, and removes per-client flexibility. If Peer 1 wants to mute Peer 3 just for themselves, they can't — the streams are already baked together. Everyone sees and hears the same combined feed.\n\n## SFU — Selective Forwarding Unit\n\n**SFU (Selective Forwarding Unit)** is what powers Google Meet, Zoom, and virtually all modern video conferencing at scale. The architecture is similar to MCU — all peers connect to a central server and upload their stream — but the server's behavior is fundamentally different.\n\nInstead of mixing streams, the SFU acts as a **pass-through pipe**. It receives each peer's raw stream and forwards them all separately to every other peer. If there are four participants, each peer receives three individual streams (one per other participant), not one blended feed.\n\nThis has major benefits:\n\n- **Low server CPU** — no transcoding or mixing, just routing raw packets.\n- **Client-side control** — since each participant's stream arrives separately, the receiving client decides how to render them. You can mute a specific person just for yourself, pin someone's video to full screen, or hide a participant — all without touching the server.\n- **Selective consumption** — the server can decide which streams to forward to which clients (e.g., only streams from the same meeting room), hence \"selective\" in the name.\n\nThis is why in Google Meet you can resize individual tiles, pin a speaker, or locally mute someone without affecting what others hear.\n\n## MediaSoup — Building Your Own SFU\n\nIf you want to implement your own SFU, **MediaSoup** is a widely-used open-source low-level SFU library for Node.js. Piyush has worked extensively with it and describes its internal architecture — Workers, Transports, and Routers — as particularly well-designed. It is not beginner-friendly but gives you full control over stream routing.\n\n## Summary\n\n| Approach | Server Needed | CPU Cost | Client Flexibility | Scalability |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| P2P (WebRTC) | No | Low | High | 2 peers only |\n| Mesh P2P | No | High (per peer) | High | Poor |\n| MCU | Yes | Very High | None | Moderate |\n| SFU | Yes | Low | Full | Production-grade |\n\nFor any real multi-party video product, SFU is the answer."
---

A deep dive into how multi-party video conferencing systems like Zoom, Google Meet, and Discord are architected under the hood. Piyush Garg explains the three core approaches — WebRTC peer-to-peer, SFU (Selective Forwarding Unit), and MCU (Multipoint Control Unit) — and the trade-offs between them at scale.
